text: This video is of a laparoscopic repair of a traumatic diaphragmatic hernia. The patient is a fifty year old male with a traumatic diaphragmatic hernia and a past medical history significant for hereditary hemorrhagic telangiectasias with subsequent development of severe pulmonary hypertension and symptomatic right-sided heart failure. The patient had a penetrating knife wound of the left flank thirty years prior with progressive symptoms of dyspnea and chest pain with recurrent pleural effusions requiring thoracentesis. His pulmonary hypertension was optimized with medical therapy and after cardiac anesthesia clearance he was consented for a laparoscopic diaphragmatic hernia repair.

The preoperative CT scan reveals a left lateral diaphragmatic defect containing incarcerated omentum and collapse of the left lower lobe. In the operating room, the patient is positioned in the right lateral decubitus position. An 11mm camera port is placed in the left upper quadrant, and three working ports are positioned along the left costal margin. The left diaphragmatic defect is visualized, confirming the presence of the majority of the patient's omentum.

The surgeon identifies and divides the peritoneal lining along the lateral rim of the diaphragmatic defect using sharp dissection or cautery, avoiding injury to the hernia contents. After mobilizing and dividing the peritoneal attachments, steady downward traction is applied to gently reduce the hernia contents back into the abdomen. For defects with a small neck, the diaphragm is divided with cautery to enlarge the defect, facilitating the reduction of hernia contents.

The surgeon explores the left chest laparoscopically, encountering bloody loculated effusions from the incarcerated hernia contents, which are removed using a suction irrigation device. The remaining pleural effusion is suctioned out. The left lower lobe, initially collapsed, is re-expanded through preferential ventilation using a double lumen tube.

The surgeon uses cautery to detach the final omental attachments from the posterior diaphragm. The diaphragmatic defect, measuring approximately three to four centimeters, is closed primarily using a laparoscopic suturing device with nonabsorbable running suture. If necessary, nonabsorbable composite mesh is used to reinforce the closure. The abdomen is desufflated, and a twenty-eight French chest tube is placed.
